exemplar.title: Investigating the Relationship between Age and 100-Meter Sprint Times through Mathematical Modelling and Regression

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Relationship between Age and 100-Meter Sprint Times through Mathematical Modelling and Regression

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The exploration follows a clear narrative structure with linked sections (introductionâdataâmodellingâconclusion)
Headings, sub-headings and page breaks provide a well-organised skeleton
Tables and figures are generally located near their explanation, aiding reader navigation
weaknesses: Occasional digressions (physiological commentary, repeated definitions of r and RÂ²) disrupt the thread
Appendix and citation blocks interrupt the flow; some results appear before explanation
Verbose narrative and tangential material reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation is coherent and logically developed (Band 3) with a clear structure, but lapses in conciseness and some organizational hiccups (redundant definitions, misplaced appendices) prevent it from achieving the highest band.

strengths: Consistent use of LaTeX-typeset notation for functions, integrals, r and RÂ²
Effective use of multiple representations (tables, Desmos plots, integral diagrams) with clear labels
Logical sequence of explanations supported by equations and figures
weaknesses: Minor notational slips (missing differential in integrals, occasional typos)
SWR method introduced without an explicit formula before referring to integrals
Variable notation could be formalized earlier and citations need consistent styling
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Notation and representations are mostly consistent and communicate the mathematics clearly (Band 3). While effective, there are minor clarity and completeness issues that keep it from the top band.

strengths: The comparison of male vs female models and the creation of a shoulder-to-waist ratio show independent thought
The exploration is grounded in the studentâs personal interest in running and the Olympics
Applying integral calculus to image-derived body areas demonstrates initiative
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on Desmos auto-regression and standard formulas limits originality
No alternative measurement techniques or expanded data collection were attempted
Creativity is good but stops short of an outstanding personal innovation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is clear evidence of personal engagement and independent initiative (Band 2), but the work does not reach the exceptional creativity or depth required for the highest band.

strengths: Acknowledges model limitations and measurement error
Notes the need for more data and mentions future work
weaknesses: Reflection is superficial and descriptive rather than probing
Over-reliance on RÂ² without critique of overfitting or sample size
No substantive discussion of residuals, outliers or statistical remedies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reflection shows only limited critical analysis and evaluation (Band 1). Comments remain at a surface level without the depth or insight needed for higher bands.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (regression, correlation, definite integrals) directly supports the research aim
Methodology is well explained (Pearsonâs r, percentage error) and linked to conclusions
Calculus and statistical tools are applied effectively beyond a minimal level
weaknesses: Minor computational inaccuracies (omitted dx, scale-factor errors)
Vertex coordinates for the quadratic model are not calculated
No residual analysis or hypothesis testing to deepen model validation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics is used effectively and appropriately for SL level (Band 4). While not flawless, the choice and application of techniques demonstrate a solid, above-average command of course-level mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations are generally sequential and supported by equations/figures.  The student states what each statistic means and links it to conclusions.  Some paragraphs are wordy, yet the mathematical argument remains intelligible.  This fits âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ.

Calculations of r, RÂ², regression equations and definite integrals are largely accurate; minor slips (missing dx, inaccurate scale factors) appear but do not undermine overall correctness.  Explanations indicate good but not flawless understanding, matching Band 3.

The student employs tables, Desmos scatter plots, regression equations and integral diagrams.  These representations are relevant and usually labelled; they help the reader visualise relationships.  While the shoulder-area shading could be clearer, overall use is âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The investigation follows a recognisable narrative (introduction â data collection â modelling â conclusion).  Each section links reasonably to the next and the reader can follow how the student moves from ageâtime data to SWR calculations.  However, the argument occasionally drifts (e.g., long physiological digressions, repeated definitions of r and RÂ²) and the modelling of shoulder-to-waist ratio breaks the earlier logical thread.  These lapses prevent the work from reaching the âhighly coherentâ standard but overall coherence is better than merely âsomeâ.  Band 3 is therefore most appropriate.

Using integral calculus over images to approximate body areas is a creative idea and shows initiative.  Limitations are acknowledged but no alternative measurement technique is attempted, so creativity is good rather than exceptional.

Analysis of results is basic: the student equates larger RÂ² with âbetter modelâ without critiquing small sample size, heteroscedasticity, or overfitting.  Discussion of SWR findings is largely speculative.  This fits âbasic analysisâ.

Evaluation notes measurement error and suggests collecting more data, but does not quantify impact or propose concrete statistical remedies.  Future work is mentioned generically.  Consequently, evaluation is âlimitedâ.

The student provides surface-level reflection (e.g., âage alone does not contributeâ, âmany other factors affect performanceâ) but stops at description.  There is little probing of why the models fail or succeed, nor of data-set limitations beyond brief comments.  Hence only âlimited reflectionâ is evident.

Notation for functions, integrals, r and RÂ² is largely correct and typeset in LaTeX.  Minor slips occur (missing differential in some integrals, âwaitâ for âwaistâ) but they seldom impede meaning.  Hence âmostly consistentâ use of appropriate language.

The exploration is clearly motivated by the studentâs personal interest in running and the Olympics, and a self-designed SWR method is applied.  This demonstrates a âclear evidence of personal approachâ, though it stops short of highly innovative data collection or model creation.

At 20 pages the account is verbose: identical explanations of r, RÂ² and regression appear for male and female data; lengthy quotations from sources are reproduced; the appendix replicates raw tables already summarised.  Irrelevant narrative (Olympics publicity, personal running anecdotes) is frequent.  Nevertheless, most paragraphs do relate to the central aim, so the work is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ â Band 2.

Linear, quadratic and cubic regression, Pearson correlation, integral area calculations and scale-factor similarity are all relevant to the stated aims.  Although the dataset is small, the mathematics chosen is appropriate and used beyond a minimal level.  Relevance is therefore âused effectivelyâ â Band 4.

Choosing to compare male vs female data, testing three models, and devising a shoulder-to-waist approach show a measure of independent thought beyond textbook examples.  However, execution relies heavily on Desmos auto-regression and standard formulas; originality is noticeable but not outstanding â Band 2.

Headings, sub-headings and page breaks give the exploration a clear skeleton, and tables/figures are generally located near the related text.  Introduction, body and conclusion are all present.  Nonetheless, the appendix and citation blocks interrupt the flow and some results appear before their explanations (e.g., SWR integrals precede scale-factor justification).  Thus organisation is âwell organised with clear structureâ rather than âexceptionalâ.

Regression, coefficients and definite integrals are squarely within the AA SL syllabus and are applied correctly.  The work does not extend to HL-level techniques (e.g., multivariable regression, hypothesis testing), so the mathematics is âcommensurate with course levelâ but not beyond â Band 3.

annotations.comment: Introduce notation for variables early (e.g., let x = age, y = time) to clarify subsequent equations.
The title clearly states the focus on age and sprint times, guiding the reader into the investigation.
The title page lacks candidate name, number, and centre details; include these to meet IB formatting requirements.
Page count on the title page is unnecessary; include a separate cover sheet with this administrative information instead.
The opening paragraph contains narrative on Olympics publicity which is tangential; focus more tightly on ageâperformance relationship.
Clear personal engagement is demonstrated by linking the exploration to the studentâs running experience and the Paris Olympics.
The citation for Korhonen lacks page or year context; ensure in-text citations follow an established style consistently.
The hypothesis indicates peak performance âabove 26â but lacks a precise value; specify an expected age or inject model parameters to sharpen the aim.
The methodology section effectively outlines the use of correlation coefficients and percentage error to assess model fit.
The description of the SWR method lacks an explicit formula before referring to integrals; present the SWR definition mathematically first.
Only applying SWR modeling to top three finalists limits generalizability; justify this choice or include a broader sample.
The transition from regression analysis to SWR modeling is abrupt and disrupts logical flow; insert a signpost to maintain coherence.
Including both raw and summarized data tables helps the reader verify results and follow calculations.
Table 1.1 caption is incomplete and confusing; clarify the title and data source directly above the table.
Consolidate repeated definitions of r and RÂ² appearing later to improve conciseness and avoid redundancy.
The explanation of Pearsonâs r value is clear and appropriately linked to model strength.
The interpretation equates low correlation directly with model inadequacy without considering sample size or outliers.
Discuss potential outliers in the scatter plot and consider reporting residuals to deepen analysis.
The quadratic modelâs discussion omits coordinates of the vertex; include vertex form or calculation for clarity.
Comparing linear, quadratic, and cubic models shows initiative and independent exploration of different fit options.
Consider calculating and interpreting residual sum of squares to quantify model error beyond RÂ².
The positive slope interpretation implies times increase with age for all ranges, which contradicts expected physiological peak; address this inconsistency.
Plot residuals or examine scatter to check if a piecewise model might better capture performance trends.
Figures are consistently labeled and referenced, facilitating clear mathematical communication.
The raw data table for female sprinters is clearly presented with consistent decimal places.
The reliance on RÂ² alone to select a model fails to consider overfitting in higher-degree polynomials.
Incorporate adjusted RÂ² or information criteria to support model choice more robustly.
